questions.question: A creator's application funnel shows 400 applications started but only 160 completed. What is the completion rate and what does it indicate?

options.text: 40% -- below the 60-80% benchmark, indicating the form has significant problems
40% -- acceptable for high-ticket offers where thorough applications are expected
60% -- at the lower end of the healthy range
40% -- above average since most application forms see 20-30% completion

questions.explanation: 160/400 = 40% completion rate. The benchmark is 60-80%, so 40% indicates significant problems with the form. Common causes include too many questions (more than 8-10), open-ended questions requiring long answers early in the form, or technical friction like slow loading or mobile-unfriendly design.

questions.question: Research shows that leads contacted within 5 minutes of expressing interest are how much more likely to enter the sales process compared to leads contacted after 30 minutes?
questions.explanation: According to the InsideSales.com research cited in the lesson, leads contacted within 5 minutes are 21 times more likely to enter the sales process than leads contacted after 30 minutes. This is why speed-to-lead is one of the most underrated metrics in application funnels.

questions.question: In the funnel math example, reducing application completion rate from 75% to 40% (with all other stages unchanged) reduced revenue from $130,000 to $70,000 on 1,000 visitors. What does this demonstrate?

options.text: Application forms should be eliminated entirely to remove friction
A single leaky stage can cost more than half your potential revenue, making stage-by-stage tracking essential
Close rate is more important than application completion rate
You should only optimize the final stage of the funnel since that directly affects revenue

questions.explanation: The example shows that one weak stage (40% vs 75% completion) caused a $60,000 revenue loss from the same traffic. This demonstrates why tracking every stage matters -- if you only measure end-to-end conversion, you cannot identify which stage is leaking and will waste effort fixing the wrong thing.

questions.question: Map out your current application or sales funnel stage by stage. For each stage, estimate your conversion rate (even a rough guess is better than nothing). Where is your biggest drop-off, and what is one specific fix you could implement this week to address it?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the student identified distinct stages in their funnel with conversion rate estimates. Check if they correctly identified their biggest drop-off point and proposed a specific, actionable fix (not a vague improvement). If they do not have an application funnel, assess whether they identified the equivalent stages in their sales process.
